source.title: SIGTERM Connection Drain and Graceful Shutdown Playbook
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Purpose
-------
This playbook describes the operational procedure, runtime integrations, and verification steps for performing graceful shutdowns of inference runtime workers in response to SIGTERM (or equivalent orchestration signals). It covers: connection draining, inflight request completion, kernel-scheduler handoff, KV cache persistence, observability checks, and rollback. The goal is to avoid user-facing 5xx/timeout spikes and to preserve SLOs for p99 latency and error rate during deployments and preemptions.

Scope
-----
- Applies to: runtime worker processes (redwood-runtime-worker), shard proxies (kv-shard-proxy), and the request-router service.
- Deployment modes: Hosted API, Dedicated nodes, and Private single-tenant installs (behavior differs slightly; see "Mode differences" below).

Ownership and Contacts
---------------------
- Runbook owner: Runtime Operations (runtime-ops@redwood.internal)
- Primary on-call: eng-sre/runtime team (page: /oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process)
- Escalation: Runtime Lead — Arjun Patel, and Kernel Scheduling Lead — Lina Gomez

Key SLOs to preserve during shutdown
----------------------------------
- p99 request latency for text-generation endpoints: 300ms target degradation window
- 5xx error rate: < 0.5% global increase tolerated during drain windows
- Maximum queue length per worker: 8 (if exceeded, router will re-route)

Principles
----------
- Prefer draining over immediate process kill. Allow inflight generation to finish up to a configurable soft timeout (default 10s).
- Prevent new requests: stop accepting new upstream connections and signal the router to stop routing new sessions to the instance.
- Give kernel scheduler and batcher time to flush batched work and spill KV cache if storing to shared cache is necessary.
- Instrument every step with metrics and trace spans so SREs can verify correctness automatically.

Sequence: Graceful Shutdown (recommended)
----------------------------------------
1) Orchestrator signals SIGTERM to redwood-runtime-worker. This may be from k8s (preStop), autoscaler, or a manual kill.
2) Worker transitions to DRAINING state and publishes event: runtime.instance.drain_start (tags: instance_id, node_pool, model).
3) Worker removes itself from service-discovery / load-balancer via Router API: POST /router/v1/instances/{id}/set-drain with payload {"drain": true, "soft_timeout_ms": 10000}.
4) Router ceases assigning new requests. Any sessions already routed continue until completion. Router emits router.instance.eviction and begins rebalancing.
5) Kernel scheduler is notified to finish current kernel runs but avoid scheduling new batches for this worker. Scheduler sets kernel-credit to 0 for new kernels on that worker.
6) If a batch is currently running, the runtime allows up to soft_timeout_ms for completion. If batch overruns, the batcher signals preemption checkpoints: write KV cache to ephemeral storage (if configured) and surface incomplete token count in traces.
7) After inflight work completes or the soft timeout expires, worker flushes KV cache shards that are configured for warm-start persistence. Use CLI: /usr/local/bin/rw-kv-flush --shard <id> --dest /mnt/ephemeral/kvcache/<id>.
8) Worker publishes runtime.instance.drain_complete and transitions to SHUTTING_DOWN.
9) Perform final telemetry flush: flush traces, metrics, and event logs to central collector (otel-collector:4317).
10) Worker exits with code 0. Orchestrator can then terminate the VM or detach the GPU.

Abort path (forced kill)
-----------------------
- If a SIGKILL or 30s post-SIGTERM timeout occurs, worker must attempt one last telemetry flush. The forced-kill event runtime.instance.forced_kill must be emitted with reason and inflight_count.
- The router should detect dropped connections and increment a router.eviction.dropped counter; automated mitigation routes traffic to fallback model variants within 2s.

Mode differences
----------------
- Hosted API (multi-tenant): router enforces aggressive re-routing. soft_timeout_ms default=10s
- Dedicated (reserved capacity): more lenient soft timeout, default=30s, because throttling downstream is under customer contract.
- Private (on-prem): operator-configurable; document recommends aligning to Dedicated defaults unless operator constraints require shorter windows.

Kernel-scheduler interactions and knobs to tune
-----------------------------------------------
- kernel_credit_window: how many kernel launches are permitted during the drain window. Set to 0 to prevent new launches.
- batcher_drain_mode: when true, batcher stops aggregating new requests into kernels destined for this worker.
- preempt_checkpoint_interval_ms: interval for writing a consistent preemption checkpoint inside long-running kernels (recommended 500ms for generation workloads > 2s).
- Example config YAML snippet (runtime config):

  ---
  runtime: 
    shutdown:
      soft_timeout_ms: 10000
      flush_kv: true
      preempt_checkpoint_interval_ms: 500
    scheduler: 
      kernel_credit_window: 0
      batcher_drain_mode: true
  ---

Observability and verification checklist (pre- and post-shutdown)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Pre-shutdown (automated):
- Verify instance has active sessions: gauge runtime.sessions.active > 0. If none, immediate shutdown OK.
- Emit drain_start event and confirm router acknowledgement (router.instance.ack_drain == true).
- Record current p99 latency baseline for the model.

During shutdown:
- Monitor: runtime.inflight.count, runtime.kernel.active_batches, kvcache.flush.latency_ms.
- Traces: Ensure spans include drain_start and drain_complete and that root span duration <= soft_timeout_ms + 2s.
- Alerts to watch: router.eviction.dropped > 0 persist for > 10s (trigger paged alert).

Post-shutdown verification (automated smoke):
1) Run a canary traffic test that sends 50 requests distributed across same model and compares p99 latency to baseline. Pass criteria: p99 <= baseline + 20%.
2) Confirm no unexplained 5xx spike: metrics.delta(api.errors.5xx) < 0.5%
3) Check telemetry logs for runtime.instance.forced_kill — if present, trigger a postmortem.

Runbook: manual operator procedure (fast path)
-------------------------------------------
1) On-call receives alert about scheduled maintenance or faulty node.
2) SSH to controller and note instance id: curl -s http://metadata.internal/instance-id
3) Trigger drain via Router API: curl -X POST "https://router.internal/api/v1/instances/<id>/action" -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" -d '{"action":"drain","soft_timeout_ms":10000}'
4) Wait for runtime.instance.drain_complete event (timeout: 60s). If not complete, inspect runtime logs: journalctl -u redwood-runtime-worker -n 200
5) If kernel batch stuck > soft_timeout_ms: request kernel-team to inspect live kernel stacks: /usr/local/bin/rw-kernel-dump --pid <pid> > /tmp/kernel-dump.txt
6) If unable to recover, trigger failover: tag instance as unhealthy in service-discovery and scale up replacement nodes via autoscaler.
7) After replacement nodes are up and passing smoke tests, decommission old instance.

Testing and validation matrix
---------------------------
- Unit tests: runtime integration tests simulate SIGTERM and validate sequence transitions.
- Integration tests: run on staging release lanes with variable latency models including long-running generation (20–60s) and check KV flush artifacts land in expected S3 prefix s3://staging-runtime-kv/<instance>/
- Chaos tests: scheduled preemptions injected at random phases of kernel execution (see: benchmarking-and-perf-suite/synthetic-chaos-stress-profiles-2026)

Dashboards and Alerts
---------------------
Recommended dashboards:
- Instance Drain Overview: shows runtime.instance.* events, inflight counts, kernel active batches, kv flush latency, and router ack status.
- Eviction Impact Dashboard: p99 delta vs baseline, error rate delta, dropped connection count.

Suggested alerts (pager for PRD-impacting metrics):
- router.eviction.dropped > 10 in 60s AND p99 delta > 25%
- runtime.instance.forced_kill > 0
- kvcache.flush.latency_ms > 5000

Post-incident analysis (if forced_kill observed)
--------------------------------------------
- Owner: Runtime incident responder. Timeline to collect: kernel dumps, router routing table at time of event, traces with drain tags, and orchestrator event stream (k8s events or cloud provider preemption log).
- Metrics to include: inflight_count, active_batches, kernel_wait_time_histogram, and router.retry_count.
- Output: short-form incident report with root cause, mitigation, and action items (file to /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/incident-review/).

Compatibility notes and caveats
-----------------------------
- Some older models that rely on pinned KV state may need longer soft_timeouts; consult model-onboarding docs before changing defaults.
- In air-gapped Private installs, ensure the KV flush destination is reachable (NFS or operator-provided S3-compatible store).
- Draining behavior for GPU co-located processes: if multiple runtime workers share a GPU, prefer in-place workload migration with kernel scheduler support rather than draining all workers simultaneously.

Appendix: Helpful CLI snippets
----------------------------
- Trigger router drain (example):
  curl -X POST "https://router.internal/api/v1/instances/<id>/action" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"action":"drain","soft_timeout_ms":10000}'

- Check last drain events for instance:
  curl -s "https://events.internal/query?instance_id=<id>&event=runtime.instance.drain_*" | jq '.'

- KV flush (example):
  /usr/local/bin/rw-kv-flush --shard 4 --dest s3://runtime-kv-backups/staging/<id> --sync

- Kernel dump (debug only):
  /usr/local/bin/rw-kernel-dump --pid $(pgrep -f redwood-runtime-worker) > /tmp/kernel-$(date +%s).dump

References
----------
- Runtime architecture overview: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/request-multiplexing-backpressure-architecture-2026
- Kernel scheduler contracts: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/runtime-priority-decoupling-and-preemptive-shepherding-2026
- Eviction and eviction strategies: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/gpu-affinity-and-eviction-strategies-2026
- Chaos and stress profiles: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/synthetic-chaos-stress-profiles-2026

Change log
----------
- 2024-11-01: Created by Maya Chen (initial draft).
- 2025-01-10: Added kernel-scheduler knobs and CLI examples (reviewer: Lina Gomez).
- 2025-02-20: Finalized mode-specific defaults and added postmortem checklist.

  curl -s "https://events.internal/query?instance_id=<id>&event=runtime.instance.drain_*" | jq '.'

- KV flush (example):
  /usr/local/bin/rw-kv-flush --shard 4 --dest s3://runtime-kv-backups/staging/<id> --sync

- Kernel dump (debug only):
  /usr/local/bin/rw-kernel-dump --pid $(pgrep -f redwood-runtime-worker) > /tmp/kernel-$(date +%s).dump

References
----------
- Runtime architecture overview: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/request-multiplexing-backpressure-architecture-2026
- Kernel scheduler contracts: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/runtime-priority-decoupling-and-preemptive-shepherding-2026
- Eviction and eviction strategies: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/gpu-affinity-and-eviction-strategies-2026
- Chaos and stress profiles: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/synthetic-chaos-stress-profiles-2026

Change log
----------
- 2024-11-01: Created by Maya Chen (initial draft).
- 2025-01-10: Added kernel-scheduler knobs and CLI examples (reviewer: Lina Gomez).
- 2025-02-20: Finalized mode-specific defaults and added postmortem checklist.

source.title: cost-provenance-and-redispatch-benchmark-spec-2024
source.author: Marisol Vega
source.content:
Overview:

This document defines a benchmark specification and playbook for measuring cost provenance and redispatch overhead across the Redwood serving runtime. The goal is to produce reproducible, SLO-driven measurements that connect observed token-level cost and latency back to routing/redispatch decisions, kernel scheduling characteristics, and model-variant selection. Results are used to validate cost-accounting telemetry, guide scheduler tuning, and certify fallback/redispatch policies before rollout.

Audience:
- Runtime engineers (kernel/scheduler)
- Performance/benchmarking team
- SRE and capacity planning
- Product owners for routing and pricing

Goals and success criteria:
- Accurate attribution: >=95% of per-request cost is attributed to a provenance chain (routing -> model-variant -> kernel profile -> quantization profile).
- Redispatch overhead measurement: measure median and 95th percentile end-to-end latency and token-cost delta introduced by redispatch within the runtime.
- Sensitivity matrix: quantify how scheduler preemption, slot admission, and KV-cache eviction influence cost per token and end-user latency across three canonical workloads (chat, completions, and embeddings).
- Benchmarks are automatable and checkpointed for regression detection in CI.

Scope:
- In-scope: Hosted and Dedicated runtime modes; synthetic and shadowed production traffic; benchmarking against model variants including full FP16, int8, and mixed-quantized kernels; network redispatch and region failover routes.
- Out-of-scope: Private (air-gapped) installations unless explicitly requested by a customer experiment.

Terminology (short):
- Cost provenance: end-to-end mapping from a user request to chargeable units and underlying kernel resource usage that produced them.
- Redispatch: runtime action that reroutes a request mid-flight or before execution (e.g., fallback model variant, different GPU pool, region).
- Kernel profile: measured execution characteristics (per-token FLOPs, memory bandwidth, kernel latency distribution) for a specific model+quantization combo.
- Attribution trace: structured telemetry event that links request id -> routing decisions -> kernel ids -> cost bucket.

Benchmark suite overview:
- Suite name: cp-redispatch-v1
- Scenarios:
  - Steady-state chat load (95% short-turn tokens, 5% long-turn outliers)
  - Burst completion load (many short requests with high concurrency)
  - Embedding throughput (large batch sizes, variable tokenization overhead)
  - Redispatch storm (induced capacity shortfall triggering model and region fallbacks)

Per-scenario tests (summary):
1) Steady-state chat + shadow-redispatch
  - Purpose: measure cost drift when prefix caching hits are present and occasional redispatches occur due to model rollouts.
  - Synthetic payloads: 5k unique prompt templates, mean tokens per request=120, entropy skew to create KV-cache hit ratio ~40%.

2) Burst completion + preemption interference
  - Purpose: measure scheduling preemption latency and cost increase when high-priority bursts preempt long-running batches.
  - Synthetic payloads: 200 concurrent long completions (mean tokens=800) with interleaved 2k short requests.

3) Embedding throughput tuning
  - Purpose: validate batching kernel throughput and tokenization cost attribution, especially when redispatch moves batches between GPU types.
  - Synthetic payloads: 1M short texts (2–32 tokens) streamed in adjustable batch sizes.

4) Redispatch storm
  - Purpose: stress routing and fallback logic, measure end-to-end overhead and cost provenance fidelity under frequent redispatches and partial KV-cache invalidation.
  - Synthetic payloads: ramping concurrency to 3x steady-state, force a configured region capacity-limit at 70% to trigger tiered fallback.

Harness, config, and reproducibility
- Run harness: use benchmark-orchestrator (internal) with the following flags and experiment manifest pattern. All experiments must be run from the ci/benchmarks pipeline with the cp-redispatch-v1 manifest.

Sample manifest table (plain text):
| field | example | description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| experiment_id | cp-redispatch-chat-20241201 | unique id for run |
| model_variants | redwood-v2-7b-fp16, redwood-v2-7b-int8 | comma-separated target variants |
| routing_policy | latency-first, cost-first, tiered-fallback | policy under test |
| concurrency_profile | steady|burst|burst then decay | shorthand profile |
| kv_cache_seed | 0.4 | target cache hit fraction |
| simulated_region_limits | eu-west-1:70% | force capacity to trigger fallback |

CLI example (synth harness):
- ./bench-runner --manifest manifests/cp-redispatch-chat-2024.yaml --out-dir /tmp/cp-run --tags cp-redispatch,canary

Telemetry and cost provenance traces
- Required trace fields (JSON event, exported to telemetry pipeline):
  - request_id (string)
  - experiment_id (string)
  - route_decision_chain (list of route ids)
  - model_variant_id (string)
  - kernel_profile_id (string)
  - quantization_profile (string)
  - redispatch_events (list of {timestamp, from_route, to_route, reason})
  - kvcache_hit (bool)
  - tokens_generated (int)
  - effective_charge_units (float)
  - start_ts, end_ts (ISO8601)
  - per-token_latency_ms_distribution (summary)
  - tenant_id (string)
  - cpu_ns / gpu_ns attribution buckets (floats)

Note: telemetry export must include consistent sampling keys so bench analysis can correlate kernel telemetry with request-level traces. Use trace_key = sha256(request_id + experiment_id).

Metrics and SLOs (primary):
| metric | target | acceptance criteria |
|-------|--------:|---------------------|
| provenance_coverage (%) | >=95 | percent of requests with a complete attribution chain |
| redispatch_latency_add_ms (median) | <=12 ms | median added latency when a redispatch occurs |
| redispatch_latency_add_ms (p95) | <=120 ms | 95th percentile added latency |
| per_token_cost_variation (%) | <=7 | delta in cost-per-token between baseline and redispatched path |
| kernel_preemption_overhead (p95 ms) | <=80 | p95 preemption-induced latency observed in kernel traces |

Data collection and analysis steps (numbered):
1) Provision benchmark cluster matching the target mode (hosted or dedicated) and attach synthetic traffic generator.
2) Seed KV-cache and warm models as described in manifest.
3) Run baseline (no redispatch) for 30 minutes and collect telemetry.
4) Run redispatch-enabled policy for same profile for 30 minutes. Ensure experiment ids are distinct.
5) Export raw traces to analysis bucket and run the cp-analysis notebook to synthesize provenance coverage and redispatch overhead reports.
6) Compare baseline vs redispatch runs to produce a delta report with breakdowns by tenant, model_variant, and kernel_profile.

Failure modes and investigation checklist:
- Low provenance coverage (<95%): verify telemetry pipeline sampling keys, check agent instrumentation versions on runtimes, and ensure kernel_profile_id is surfaced.
- High redispatch p95: check region queue depths, syscall wait spikes, and kernel preemption counters. Run kernel trace diff (see Appendix).
- Unexpected cost delta: inspect quantization mismatch, model-variant selection mismatch, and hidden reheating (model cold starts).

Runbook / escalation (short):
- On detection of >10% regression in per_token_cost_variation across a release gate:
  1) Open incident in Jira with label bench/cp-redispatch.
  2) Attach raw run manifest and analysis output.
  3) Page runtime-oncall and scheduling owners.
  4) If regression correlates with kernel change, execute canary rollback and re-run the experiment.

Rollout safety checklist for routing changes that enable redispatch by default:
- Have validated cp-redispatch-v1 experiments for both hosted and dedicated modes.
- SLO fast-fail gates: provenance_coverage >=95% and redispatch p95 <= defined threshold.
- Observability hooks: live dashboards for route_decision_count, redispatch_rate, and per-tenant cost-delta.
- Canary window: 48 hours with shadowing enabled and 5% traffic pilot.

Ownership and contacts:
- Benchmark owner: Marisol Vega (benchmarking@redwood.com)
- Runtime scheduling: Ethan Cole (scheduling@redwood.com)
- Telemetry and cost accounting: Priya Natarajan (telemetry@redwood.com)

Appendix A — Example telemetry snippet (JSON):
{"request_id":"r-12345","experiment_id":"cp-redispatch-chat-20241201","route_decision_chain":["route-a","route-b"],"model_variant_id":"redwood-v2-7b-int8","kernel_profile_id":"kp-int8-v2-7b-cuda-1","quantization_profile":"int8-qs0","redispatch_events":[{"timestamp":"2024-12-01T12:02:03.456Z","from_route":"route-a","to_route":"route-b","reason":"capacity-fallback"}],"kvcache_hit":false,"tokens_generated":142,"effective_charge_units":0.0042,"start_ts":"2024-12-01T12:02:03.100Z","end_ts":"2024-12-01T12:02:05.200Z","per_token_latency_ms_distribution":{"p50":6.2,"p95":28.3},"tenant_id":"t-acme","cpu_ns":12000000,"gpu_ns":44000000}

(Note: the actual file above contains the full doc body; the analysis tooling expects the doc content in the 'content' field.)

Overview:

This document defines a benchmark specification and playbook for measuring cost provenance and redispatch overhead across the Redwood serving runtime. The goal is to produce reproducible, SLO-driven measurements that connect observed token-level cost and latency back to routing/redispatch decisions, kernel scheduling characteristics, and model-variant selection. source.title: Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)
source.author: Hiro Tanaka
source.content:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is confirmed or likely (see SEV process page).
* Runtime oncall has identified *candidate rollback target* (prior runtime release tag).
* Release engineer or infra oncall is available to execute the pipeline rollback (see CI/CD rollback doc).

h1. Ownership and roles
* Incident Commander (IC): owns the decision to rollback and comms cadence.
* Runtime oncall: validates technical root cause hypothesis and rollback target.
* Release/Infra oncall: executes the rollback in deployment tooling.
* SRE: monitors fleet-level SLOs and regional health signals.

h1. Rollback decision checklist
Before executing rollback, capture in the incident channel:
# Current runtime release identifier (e.g., runtime-release-2025.02.01)
# Intended rollback target (e.g., runtime-release-2025.01.17)
# Blast radius (Hosted regions; Dedicated clusters/tenants)
# Primary symptom (TTFT, throughput, 5xx, OOM)
# Confirm the prior release is known-good (at least 24h stable in prod)

h1. Step-by-step procedure

## 1) Freeze rollout / stop further progression
* If a rollout is in progress, *pause* it immediately (do not continue canary expansion).
* Ensure no automated progressive delivery job will re-advance the rollout.

## 2) Hosted rollback (global fleets)
Follow the CI/CD rollback doc to pin the Hosted runtime to the rollback target, then:
* Roll back in this order:
  # non-critical region (if any still on canary only)
  # remaining canary cohort
  # all regions currently on the bad release
* After each region completes, verify:
  * 5xx rate normalizes
  * GPU utilization returns to expected
  * TTFT and token/s recover to baseline

Hosted verification queries (Console):
* Dashboard: "Hosted Inference / Runtime Release Health"
* Metrics to watch: request_error_rate, ttft_p95_ms, tokens_per_sec, gpu_oom_count

## 3) Dedicated rollback (tenant clusters)
Dedicated rollbacks must consider customer-specific change windows and comms. In emergencies, proceed under the emergency change policy.
* Identify impacted Dedicated clusters/tenants:
  * Check "Dedicated / Runtime Release Adoption" dashboard
  * Confirm whether each tenant is on the bad release
* Execute rollback per cluster using the pipeline rollback procedure.
* For high-sensitivity tenants, coordinate with CS for outbound comms and approvals (see comms guide).

## 4) Post-rollback validation
* Keep the incident open until metrics are stable for 30 minutes (Hosted) / 60 minutes (Dedicated)
* Confirm request latency distributions return to baseline and no new error modes appear
* Ensure autoscaling stabilizes (watch for over-provisioning due to the regression)

h1. Guardrails and common failure modes
* If rollback target also shows elevated OOMs, stop and reassess (may be traffic pattern or model mix).
* If only one model family is impacted, consider temporarily routing to a compatible variant instead of fleet rollback (requires IC approval).
* Avoid rapid flip-flopping between releases; if you must re-roll forward, document the reason and require runtime lead approval.

h1. Required artifacts (for later review)
Capture links in the incident channel:
* CI/CD job link(s) for rollback
* Affected regions / clusters
* Metrics screenshots or dashboard permalinks
* Any customer comms sent

h1. References
* SEV process for runtime regressions (incident roles, paging, timelines)
* Runtime release pipeline rollback procedure (exact CI/CD steps)
* Emergency change policy (approvals and documentation)
* Customer/status comms guide (templates and approval chain)

title: Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)
preview:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is confirmed or likely (see SEV process page).
* Runtime oncall has identified *candidate rollback target* (prior runtime release tag).

* Release/Infra oncall: executes the rollback in deployment tooling.
* SRE: monitors fleet-level SLOs and regional health signals.

# Current runtime release identifier (e.g., runtime-release-2025.02.01)
# Intended rollback target (e.g., runtime-release-2025.01.17)

* Avoid rapid flip-flopping between releases; if you must re-roll forward, document the reason and require runtime lead approval.

* Customer/status comms guide (templates and approval chain)
